name: Campania
history: Campania has been inhabited since ancient times, serving as a hub for Greek colonizers who founded cities like Neapolis (Naples) and the magnificent temples at Paestum. The region flourished under Roman rule, and Pompeii and Herculaneum became wealthy resort towns before being frozen in time by the eruption of Vesuvius in 79 AD — providing the most complete picture of Roman daily life ever discovered. During the medieval period, Naples became one of Europe's largest cities and capital of successive kingdoms — Norman, Hohenstaufen, Angevin, and Aragonese — accumulating extraordinary art and architecture. The Kingdom of the Two Sicilies, based in Naples, was absorbed into unified Italy in 1861, a transition that many Neapolitans still view with ambivalence. The 20th century brought both great hardship and cultural explosion: Neapolitan music, film, and cuisine achieved global influence. Today Campania is Italy's third most populous region and one of its most visited.
culturalNotes: Campania is the birthplace of pizza, mozzarella di bufala, and a deeply food-centric culture where meals are communal and centuries-old recipes are guarded jealously. Family bonds are central to Neapolitan identity, and religious festivals — especially the twice-yearly Miracle of San Gennaro, where a vial of the saint's blood is said to liquefy — are celebrated with intense communal emotion. Neapolitan culture has an extraordinary vitality and expressiveness: the city produces some of Italy's finest music, art, theatre, and football passion. The camorra organised crime network exists but is largely invisible to tourists in most areas. The Amalfi Coast and island resorts like Capri and Ischia attract an international crowd, creating a sophisticated but expensive parallel to the gritty urban authenticity of central Naples.
bestTimeToVisit: April to June and September to October offer the best weather, avoiding summer crowds and winter rain.
languageNotes: Neapolitan dialect (napoletano) is widely spoken and differs significantly from standard Italian in vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation — it was historically considered a separate language. Common expressions include 'Aggia mangià' (I need to eat), 'Jamm'' (let's go), 'Uagliò' (hey, you), and 'Comme te chiame?' (What's your name?). Neapolitans speak rapidly and expressively, and gestures carry enormous meaning — the city has its own rich gestural vocabulary. Standard Italian is universally understood. Locals are warm and forgiving of linguistic mistakes. English is common in tourist areas. Attempting even a few words of Neapolitan will delight locals.

places.name: Naples
places.description: Naples is the vibrant capital of Campania, known for its rich history, world-famous cuisine, and chaotic energy. The historic center is a UNESCO World Heritage Site filled with churches, underground ruins, and street markets.

activities.detail: Home to the finest collection of Greco-Roman artifacts in the world, including Pompeii treasures.
This iconic street cuts through the old city and is lined with shops, churches, and street food vendors.
Visit this seaside castle for panoramic views of the Bay of Naples and the historic waterfront.
Explore Greek-Roman tunnels and aqueducts beneath the city streets on a guided tour.

activities.text: Eat pizza at a historic pizzeria
activities.detail: Try Pizzeria Brandi or L'Antica Pizzeria da Michele, birthplaces of the classic Margherita.
Marvel at the breathtaking Veiled Christ sculpture and alchemical art in this 18th-century private chapel.
Tour this stunning hilltop Carthusian monastery with panoramic views of the Bay of Naples and a rich museum inside.
Wander the vibrant Spanish Quarter streets filled with street art, local shrines, and authentic Neapolitan life.

activities.text: See the Royal Palace of Naples
activities.detail: Explore the grand Palazzo Reale on Piazza del Plebiscito, home to lavish royal apartments and a historic library.
Take a short train ride on the Circumvesuviana to walk the remarkably preserved ancient Roman city buried by Vesuvius.

food.detail: The original Neapolitan pizza with San Marzano tomatoes, fresh mozzarella, and basil.
A crispy or soft shell-shaped pastry filled with sweet ricotta and semolina cream.
A slow-cooked meat sauce simmered for hours, served over pasta like ziti or rigatoni.
Strong, dark espresso served in small cups, a daily ritual for every Neapolitan.

culture.detail: Petty theft can occur in crowded markets and tourist zones, keep bags close.
Cover shoulders and knees when entering Naples' many historic churches and basilicas.
Naples traffic and street life can feel overwhelming, but going with the flow is part of the experience.

places.name: Pompeii
places.description: Pompeii is an extraordinarily preserved ancient Roman city buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It offers an unparalleled glimpse into everyday Roman life frozen in time.

activities.detail: The civic heart of Pompeii, surrounded by temples and public buildings, is the best starting point.
Poignant casts of victims caught in the eruption are displayed throughout the excavation site.

activities.text: Visit the Villa of the Mysteries
activities.detail: This well-preserved villa features stunning frescoes depicting mysterious Dionysian rituals.
The Stabian Baths are among the oldest and best-preserved Roman thermal baths in existence.
A short drive from Pompeii, the hike to the crater rim offers breathtaking views of the bay.
The best-preserved brothel in Pompeii features original stone beds and erotic frescoes above each room door.

activities.text: Explore the House of the Faun
activities.detail: One of Pompeii's largest private homes, famous for its bronze dancing faun statue and stunning Alexander mosaic replica.
The oldest surviving Roman amphitheatre in the world, built around 70 BC and capable of seating 20,000 spectators.
Pompeii's main commercial street is lined with shops, taverns, and election graffiti still visible on original stone walls.

activities.text: Visit the Garden of the Fugitives
activities.detail: An open garden displaying 13 plaster casts of victims in their final moments, the largest group found at one location.

food.detail: Sweet cherry tomatoes grown on Vesuvius slopes, used in local sauces and bruschetta.
A hearty soup of pasta and borlotti beans, a staple of Campanian countryside cooking.
Fresh buffalo mozzarella produced nearby in the Campanian plains, best eaten the same day.
A bright lemon liqueur made from local Sorrento lemons, often served chilled as a digestif.
Try the classic Margherita at a local pizzeria using wood-fired ovens, a staple of Campanian cuisine.
A crispy shell-shaped pastry filled with ricotta and candied citrus, best enjoyed fresh from a Neapolitan bakery.
A slow-cooked rich meat sauce served over pasta, a Sunday tradition deeply rooted in Campanian households.
A crisp local white wine from the Campania region, perfect paired with seafood dishes near the coast.
Tiny fried dough balls drizzled with honey and colorful sprinkles, a beloved Campanian sweet treat.

culture.detail: The site gets extremely busy by midday, especially in summer — morning entry is much more peaceful.
The ancient cobblestone streets are uneven and can be slippery, sturdy footwear is essential.
A knowledgeable guide brings context that signage alone cannot provide across the vast ruins.
Do not touch, climb, or remove any stones or artifacts from the archaeological site.
Avoid overly revealing clothing out of respect for the cultural and historical significance of the site.
Follow designated routes only, as straying off paths can damage fragile ancient structures.
The site offers little shade, so carry a water bottle and wear sunscreen or a hat, especially in summer.
Keep voices low and avoid flash photography near delicate frescoes and preserved artworks to help preserve them.

places.name: Amalfi Coast
places.description: The Amalfi Coast is a UNESCO World Heritage stretch of dramatic cliffs, pastel-colored villages, and crystal-clear turquoise waters. Towns like Positano, Ravello, and Amalfi itself are among Italy's most iconic destinations.

activities.detail: Wander the steep lanes lined with bougainvillea, boutiques, and cliffside restaurants.
The gardens of Villa Rufolo offer some of the most stunning coastal panoramas in all of Italy.

activities.text: Take a boat tour of the coast
activities.detail: Seeing the coastline from the sea reveals hidden coves and sea caves inaccessible by road.

activities.text: Hike the Path of the Gods
activities.detail: This scenic trail above the coastline offers dramatic views from Bomerano to Nocelle.
The Duomo of Sant'Andrea is a striking Arab-Norman cathedral dominating the main piazza of Amalfi.

food.detail: Fresh handmade pasta served with a generous mixed seafood sauce, a coast specialty.
A dome-shaped sponge cake filled and glazed with lemon cream, made with local citrus.
Fresh local anchovies lightly fried and served with lemon, a simple seaside classic.
A potent fermented anchovy sauce from Cetara used to dress pasta, unique to this coast.

culture.detail: The Amalfi Coast is extremely popular; summer hotels fill months ahead, especially in Positano.
The coastal road is narrow and traffic-heavy; boats and SITA buses are often faster and less stressful.
Many beaches have private lido sections with fees; look for the free public (libero) areas nearby.

places.name: Paestum
places.description: Paestum is home to three of the best-preserved ancient Greek temples in the world, set within a large archaeological park in southern Campania. The site also features a superb museum with unique painted Greek tomb frescoes.

activities.detail: This Doric temple from the 5th century BC is one of the most complete Greek temples still standing.

activities.text: Explore the National Museum of Paestum
activities.detail: The museum houses the famous Tomb of the Diver fresco and exceptional Greek painted pottery.
Parts of the original Greek-Roman city walls are still intact and walkable around the perimeter.
Overlapping Greek and Roman layers reveal the city's centuries of continuous occupation.

activities.text: Visit a local buffalo mozzarella farm
activities.detail: The Paestum plains are Italy's prime mozzarella di bufala region; farm visits offer tastings and production tours.

food.detail: Made from local water buffalo milk, eaten fresh the same morning it is produced for peak flavor.
A creamy variant of mozzarella with a soft, buttery interior, served with olive oil and bread.
Artichokes from the Paestum area are renowned; here they are fried crispy in the Roman-Jewish style.
Silky buffalo ricotta eaten with honey or on pasta, richer and creamier than cow's milk versions.

culture.detail: The ruins and museum together tell the full story; skipping either leaves the experience incomplete.
The open plain site offers little shade and summer heat can be intense; spring or autumn is far more comfortable.
Buy mozzarella directly from local caseifici (dairies) rather than supermarkets for authentic quality.

places.name: Ischia
places.description: Ischia is a volcanic island in the Bay of Naples famed for its thermal spas, lush gardens, sandy beaches, and the imposing Aragonese Castle. It offers a more relaxed and authentic alternative to the better-known Capri.

activities.detail: Poseidon Thermal Gardens and Negombo offer multiple thermal pools set amid beautiful landscaped gardens.
This dramatic medieval castle sits on a rocky islet connected to the island by a stone bridge.
The island's highest peak offers panoramic views of the entire Bay of Naples on a clear day.
The longest beach on Ischia has natural hot springs bubbling through the sand at the water's edge.
This charming fishing village near the castle is ideal for an evening stroll and fresh seafood dinner.
A stunning botanical garden with thermal pools set directly on the bay of San Montano.
Stroll past the iconic Il Fungo mushroom rock rising from the sea in this charming resort town.
Hire a small boat to discover sea caves, hidden coves, and coastal scenery unreachable by land.
Walk the car-free lanes of this picturesque waterfront village connected to the island by a narrow isthmus.
Sample indigenous Biancolella and Per'e Palummo wines grown on the island's volcanic slopes.

food.detail: Rabbit braised with white wine, tomatoes, and herbs in a clay pot, the island's signature dish.
A rich fisherman's stew of mixed local catch, best eaten at a waterfront trattoria with crusty bread.
Local fish poached in a light tomato and herb broth, a delicate and beloved coastal preparation.
A dense, flourless chocolate and almond cake widely made on the Bay of Naples islands.

culture.detail: The circular bus routes are cheap and cover the whole island; renting a scooter is also a popular option.
Popular spa parks like Poseidon fill up in summer; online booking secures entry and better prices.
Ischia produces excellent indigenous white wines; ordering local wine supports island viticulture traditions.

places.name: Caserta
places.description: Home to the magnificent Royal Palace of Caserta, a UNESCO World Heritage Site often called the Italian Versailles. The palace and its grand gardens are among the largest royal residences in the world.

activities.detail: Arrive early to beat crowds and explore the opulent state apartments.
Follow the long central axis to the waterfall fountain at the top for great views.
Drive up to this medieval hilltop village for panoramic views and quiet streets.
Visit the historic Real Setificio Borbonco to learn about Bourbon silk production.
Rent a bike inside the palace grounds to cover the vast gardens comfortably.

food.detail: The surrounding Terra di Lavoro produces some of Campania's finest buffalo mozzarella.
Rabbit stew cooked with tomatoes, herbs, and white wine, popular in the Caserta area.
Dense, rustic local bread baked in wood-fired ovens typical to this region.
A traditional Easter wheat and ricotta tart widely enjoyed across the Caserta province.

culture.detail: Timed entry slots sell out fast, especially on weekends and public holidays.
Modest clothing is appreciated when visiting the palace chapel and religious spaces.
Many local shops close on Sunday afternoons, so plan dining and shopping accordingly.

places.name: Benevento
places.description: An ancient inland city with remarkable Roman ruins including one of Italy's best-preserved triumphal arches. Benevento blends Samnite, Roman, and medieval heritage in a largely tourist-free setting.

activities.detail: Visit at golden hour when the marble reliefs glow beautifully in warm light.
One of the best-preserved Roman theaters in southern Italy, still used for summer events.
Discover artifacts from the ancient Samnite civilization that predated Roman rule.
Stroll the old quarter for Egyptian obelisk fragments and medieval churches.

activities.text: Taste Strega liqueur at Distilleria Strega
activities.detail: Join a free tour of the famous local distillery and sample the iconic yellow liqueur.

food.detail: A distinctive herbal liqueur made with 70 botanicals, invented and produced in Benevento.
Soft or hard nougat with almonds and honey, a specialty deeply tied to the city's traditions.
Egg pasta served with a rich wild boar ragu, common in the Sannio hills.
Aged stretched-curd cheese from local Podolica cows with a nutty, complex flavor.

culture.detail: Benevento is rarely crowded, so locals appreciate genuine curiosity about their heritage.
The city embraces its legend of the Streghe di Benevento witches — ask locals about it.
Most civic museums are shut on Mondays, so plan visits for Tuesday through Sunday.

places.name: Cilento Coast
places.description: A rugged and largely unspoiled coastline in the Cilento National Park featuring sea caves, crystal waters, and clifftop villages. Far less crowded than the Amalfi Coast, it offers authentic southern Italian charm.

activities.text: Boat trip to Grotta Azzurra di Capo Palinuro
activities.detail: Take a morning boat from Palinuro to see the famous sea caves in calm conditions.
Accessible only by boat or a long hike, this pristine bay is one of Italy's most beautiful.

activities.text: Hike the Cilento National Park trails
activities.detail: Try the trail from Acciaroli to Pioppi for coastal views and wildflower meadows.
A hilltop medieval village famous for its ancient olive trees and stunning sea vistas.
Walk ruins of the ancient Greek city where the philosopher Parmenides once lived.

food.detail: Hand-caught anchovies from Pisciotta, cured in salt using an ancient Greek method.
A sweet fig syrup drizzled on cheese or bread, made from local dried Cilento figs.
Fresh mozzarella wrapped and preserved in myrtle leaves, a traditional local delicacy.
Extra-virgin oil from centuries-old olive groves with a bold, peppery finish.

culture.detail: Many small beach restaurants and boat operators only accept cash in remote areas.
Parts of the coastline are protected zones where anchoring and fishing are prohibited.
Italians holiday here in August, so visit in June or September for a quieter experience.

places.name: Procida
places.description: Italy's 2022 Capital of Culture, this tiny volcanic island near Naples is famous for its pastel-colored waterfront, narrow lanes, and unhurried pace. Less developed than Capri, it retains a deeply authentic fishing village atmosphere.

activities.detail: Walk the iconic crescent-shaped fishing port lined with stacked pastel houses.
Climb to the highest point for sweeping views over the Gulf of Naples.
The island's most popular beach, calm and family-friendly on the western side.
Circle the entire tiny island in an hour and stop at hidden viewpoints along the way.

activities.text: Take a boat to Vivara nature reserve
activities.detail: Cross the bridge to this uninhabited volcanic islet and spot migratory birds.

food.detail: A giant, thick-rinded lemon unique to the island, sweeter and more fragrant than standard lemons.
A soft pastry filled with lemon cream, the island's most beloved traditional sweet.
Clam spaghetti made with just-caught local clams and local white wine.
Tender octopus salad dressed with olive oil, lemon, and parsley, served cold.

culture.detail: The fast ferry from Molo Beverello takes about 35 minutes and is very affordable.

culture.text: Day trips are possible but stay over
culture.detail: The island transforms at dusk when day-trippers leave and the real local life begins.
The island's Easter procession is one of Italy's most moving, treat it with great respect.

places.name: Sorrento
places.description: A cliff-top town on the southern edge of the Bay of Naples famous for its lemon groves, turquoise waters, and sweeping views of Vesuvius. It serves as a gateway to Capri, Pompeii, and the Amalfi Coast.

activities.text: Walk Piazza Tasso and the old town
activities.detail: Explore the buzzing main square and the tangle of medieval lanes behind it.
Free public gardens with a dramatic terrace viewpoint over the Bay of Naples.
Several family workshops offer free tastings; the Limonoro shop is a classic stop.
Descend the steep path to the original fishing port beach for calm, clear water.

activities.text: Day trip to Capri by ferry
activities.detail: Ferries run frequently from Marina Piccola; book the return in advance in summer.

food.detail: The iconic lemon liqueur, best tasted ice-cold straight from the freezer after dinner.
Potato gnocchi baked with tomato sauce, fresh basil, and bubbling local mozzarella.
A dome-shaped sponge cake soaked in lemon cream, the dessert Sorrento is famous for.
A DOP semi-hard cheese from the Sorrento peninsula with a subtle spicy finish.

culture.text: Sunset is golden from the cliffs
culture.detail: Arrive at the Marina Grande viewpoint 30 minutes before sunset for the best colors.
Prices in markets and shops are generally fixed; aggressive bargaining is frowned upon.
The single coastal road gets severely congested, so use trains or boats instead of cars.

restaurants.name: Da Michele
restaurants.description: Founded in 1870, this legendary pizzeria serves only Margherita and Marinara, perfecting them for over 150 years.

restaurants.name: Trattoria da Nennella
restaurants.description: A boisterous, cash-only trattoria in the Spanish Quarter serving authentic Neapolitan home cooking since the 1940s.

restaurants.name: Ristorante Il Buco
restaurants.description: Housed in a 15th-century wine cellar, this Michelin-starred restaurant elevates local Sorrentine ingredients with modern technique.

restaurants.name: La Caravella
restaurants.description: Opened in 1959, this Amalfi institution was the first southern Italian restaurant to earn a Michelin star, celebrating coastal cuisine.

restaurants.name: Taverna del Capitano
restaurants.address: Piazza delle Sirene 10, Marina del Cantone
restaurants.description: A Michelin-starred seafood restaurant on a secluded beach, known for pristine local catch and stunning Gulf of Naples views.

restaurants.name: Osteria Arbustico
restaurants.description: A creative osteria in the Campanian hinterland showcasing Cilento biodiversity with a tasting menu rooted in peasant tradition.
restaurants.mustOrder: Fusilli di grano arso con cicerchia

restaurants.name: Pizzeria Pepe in Grani
restaurants.description: Franco Pepe's world-renowned pizzeria in a small medieval village, celebrated for outstanding dough and hyper-local toppings.

restaurants.name: Ristorante Krèsios
restaurants.description: Giuseppe Iannotti's two-Michelin-star laboratory pushes boundaries with avant-garde tasting menus inspired by Sannio terroir.

restaurants.name: Osteria Caracol
restaurants.description: A beloved waterfront osteria in the Phlegrean Fields serving ultra-fresh seafood caught daily in the nearby volcanic lakes and sea.

restaurants.name: Locanda del Borgo
restaurants.description: A charming countryside locanda celebrating the robust flavours of Sannio with handmade pasta, local pork and Falanghina wine.

restaurants.name: Ristorante Il Principe
restaurants.description: Unique restaurant near the excavations that reconstructs ancient Roman recipes alongside refined traditional Campanian dishes.
restaurants.mustOrder: Garum di alici con farro e verdure

restaurants.name: Gerani Ristorante
restaurants.description: Relaxed family-run restaurant near the Greek temples serving Cilento classics with famous local buffalo mozzarella and fresh catch.
restaurants.mustOrder: Mozzarella di bufala con alici marinate

restaurants.name: Trattoria Vanvitelli
restaurants.description: A well-loved local trattoria near the Royal Palace of Caserta dishing up hearty Terra di Lavoro recipes unchanged for decades.
restaurants.mustOrder: Ziti spezzati al ragù di maiale

restaurants.name: Ristorante Quattro Passi
restaurants.description: Two-Michelin-star restaurant in the tiny village of Nerano, birthplace of spaghetti alle zucchine, offering refined coastal elegance.
restaurants.mustOrder: Spaghetti alla Nerano con zucchine e provolone

restaurants.name: Osteria La Chitarra
restaurants.description: A tiny, authentic Neapolitan osteria beloved by locals for honest cucina povera dishes and a convivial communal atmosphere since the 1970s.
